facade_colour: graffiti-covered lower walls, dark brick upper (barely visible)

hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that has been modified to accommodate a commercial conversion. Through its construction date and typology, each property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. With its visibly layered built form, each property is also associated with the patterns of commercial adaptation and expansion that began in the early twentieth century. Constructed as a pair, the properties have evolved over time while retaining original building features, resulting in a visibly layered built form that includes their shared roofline, paired window openings at the second storey bays and gables with attic windows and flanking brick spires. Each property has contextual value because it supports and maintains the historic mixed use character of the area and is physically, functionally, visually, and historically linked to the surrounding market area. Summary: Reference Primary Address Entrance Address(es) Number 364. 6 St. Andrew Street N/A 365. 8 St. Andrew Street N/A 193 | FO :D FO 5202 10 St. Andrew Street

facade_description: 6 St Andrew St presents a converted house-form, semi-detached facade within the Kensington Market HCD. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its converted house-form, semi-detached typology. The facade reads primarily as brick, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade is organized as a storefront base with 2 upper-window bays, using double-hung sash windows, and 2 principal entrances. HCD note: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that has been modified to accommodate a commercial conversion. Through its construction date and typology, each property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. With its visibly layered built form, each property is also associated with the patterns of commercial adaptation and expansion that began in the early twentieth century. Constructed as a pair, the properties have evolved over time while retaining original building features, resulting in a visibly layered built form that includes their shared roofline, paired window openings at the second storey bays and gables with attic windows and flanking brick spires. Each property has contextual value because it supports and maintains the historic mixed use character of the area and is physically, functionally, visually, and historically linked to the surrounding market area. Summary: Reference Primary Address Entrance Address(es) Number 364. 6 St. Andrew Street N/A 365. 8 St. Andrew Street N/A 193 | FO :D FO 5202 10 St. Andrew Street
